name: fabled gold dragon scales
shortDescription: Fabled gold dragon scales with a burnished glow that makes coin look timid beside them. Armorers, kings, and relic keepers all want them for different excuses, but every serious buyer asks for provenance before touching the price.
flavorText: “Gold scales do not shine like treasure. Treasure imitates them.”

name: scarce green dragon scales
shortDescription: Scarce green dragon scales, prized by wardens, poison-workers, and armorers who favor materials with forest-born malice in their reputation. The color can vary from moss to emerald, but the best lots carry a sheen that looks almost alive.
flavorText: “Green dragon scales smell faintly of old leaves and older schemes.”

name: scarce blue dragon scales
shortDescription: Scarce blue dragon scales valued for stormwork, ceremonial armor, and any craft meant to impress people who fear lightning. Buyers inspect the edges for true hardness, while sellers prefer to discuss thunder, altitude, and other things difficult to verify.
flavorText: “Blue scales keep the sky's arrogance in small hard pieces.”

name: scarce ice dragon scales
shortDescription: Scarce ice dragon scales with pale surfaces and a cold reputation that survives long after harvest. Northern armorers, frost mages, and collectors pay well for clean pieces, especially when the crate arrives dry, warded, and still slightly uncomfortable to hold.
flavorText: “Winter becomes negotiable when it can be weighed.”

name: scarce Glassmere dragon scales
shortDescription: Scarce Glassmere dragon scales, shimmering like broken reflections from the marshes and mirror-bright waters of their name. They are difficult stock to appraise, because the surface changes with light and every buyer believes they saw the rarest angle first.
flavorText: “Glassmere scales make honest lamps look suspicious.”

name: fabled shimmering dragon scales
shortDescription: Fabled shimmering dragon scales that seem to carry color the way water carries moonlight. Master crafters prize them for armor, regalia, and arcane inlay, though cautious merchants wrap each piece separately before arguments over which one is brightest begin.
flavorText: “They do not reflect the room; they improve its lies.”

name: fabled silver dragon scales
shortDescription: Fabled silver dragon scales with a clean noble luster that raises the tone of any auction and the suspicion of every appraiser. Temples and royal armorers compete for them, partly for their strength and partly because silver dragons make excellent legends.
flavorText: “Silver scales look like moonlight decided to keep records.”

name: scarce swamp dragon scales
shortDescription: Scarce swamp dragon scales, rougher than courtly dragon stock but respected for stubborn durability. They carry marsh color, old mud, and practical value for armorers who care less about beauty than whether a thing survives rot, rain, and teeth.
flavorText: “Swamp scales are ugly until the blade turns.”

name: eye of devastation
shortDescription: An eye of devastation, sealed and wrapped as a dangerous magical organ rather than a mere curiosity. War mages, curse scholars, and grim collectors value it highly, though even they prefer it stored facing a wall and not the ledger.
flavorText: “Some eyes do not watch the world. They remember how to ruin it.”

name: eye of draining
shortDescription: An eye of draining associated with weakening charms, leeching curses, and the kind of magic that leaves victims feeling hollow before they understand why. It is small, rare, and unpleasantly useful, making it popular with buyers who avoid giving full names.
flavorText: “It takes less than blood first. That is what makes it frightening.”

name: giant eyeball
shortDescription: A giant eyeball preserved for alchemy, monster study, or very disturbing display cabinets. Its size makes it hard to ignore and harder to pack discreetly, but mages pay well for any organ that once saw more than humans were meant to see.
flavorText: “The worst part is not that it is large. The worst part is wondering what it noticed.”

name: scarce golden eye
shortDescription: A scarce golden eye, beautiful enough to draw jewelers and strange enough to draw occultists. Some claim it came from a radiant beast, others from a warded idol, but every merchant agrees it should be cushioned well and stared at briefly.
flavorText: “Gold becomes less comforting when it looks back.”

name: great orb of eyes
shortDescription: A great orb of eyes, an unsettling prize from watchful monstrosities and forbidden vault-guardians. It interests mages, spies, and nightmare collectors, though any seller with sense covers it before the market crowd starts feeling personally inspected.
flavorText: “It sees too much to be called merchandise honestly.”

name: shining eye
shortDescription: A shining eye with a bright, unnatural gleam that suggests magic, sanctity, or a monster that ate too much of either. Buyers prize it for divination work and amulet craft, provided the glow remains steady after the bargaining begins.
flavorText: “A shining eye is rarely a symbol of comfort.”

name: fabled golden dragon
shortDescription: A fabled golden dragon claim, token, or encounter right tied to one of the most coveted creatures in merchant legend. Its value lives in proof, prestige, and danger; without all three, even gold-colored scales are just expensive boasting.
flavorText: “To trade in golden dragons is to sell the idea that survival has paperwork.”

name: scarce flying jungle drake
shortDescription: A scarce flying jungle drake record from hot canopies where green wings flash between leaves and arrows lose confidence. Its hide, feathers, teeth, or capture rights interest collectors and scouts, especially those planning routes above or through dense country.
flavorText: “The jungle grows teeth, then teaches them to fly.”

name: scarce griffon
shortDescription: A scarce griffon token tied to proud mountain hunters with talons, wings, and enough nobility to make nobles jealous. Falconers, heralds, and war colleges all bid for griffon stock, though transport contracts become expensive the moment cages are mentioned.
flavorText: “A griffon does not belong to a crest. The crest borrows its posture.”

name: scarce harpy
shortDescription: A scarce harpy encounter token or trophy mark from cliffs, ruins, and wind-torn roads where songs can become threats. Collectors and monster scholars buy the proof, while caravan masters buy the warning and ask exactly which pass should be avoided.
flavorText: “A harpy turns altitude into an argument.”

name: fabled hippogriff
shortDescription: A fabled hippogriff claim linked to a rare proud creature of wing, beak, and noble restlessness. Courts love the symbol, riders dream of the saddle, and merchants know the price rises whenever impossible animals can be described as elegant.
flavorText: “A hippogriff is what happens when pride refuses to choose a shape.”

name: large tengu
shortDescription: A large tengu token or writ associated with a towering skywise fighter, larger than the common tales and harder to ignore. Mountain courts, duelists, and collectors seek it for prestige, while practical traders ask whether it came with airspace disputes.
flavorText: “The larger the tengu, the farther down it looks at you.”

name: scarce manticore
shortDescription: A scarce manticore record, spine, or trophy claim from a predator that ruins simple hunting stories. Alchemists prize its venomous associations, lords prize the trophy, and experienced merchants prize thick gloves and clear liability clauses.
flavorText: “A manticore is proof that nature sometimes overdecorates danger.”

name: fabled phoenix
shortDescription: A fabled phoenix token tied to flame, renewal, and the frustrating business of creatures that make death look temporary. Temples, monarchs, and resurrection cults pay extravagantly for any verified trace, especially if the ash still warms the container.
flavorText: “Phoenix goods are priced by rarity, fire, and hope.”

name: raven
shortDescription: A raven token, bird, or feathered trade good associated with clever eyes, battlefield stories, and messages that seem to arrive before comfort does. Common enough in the wild, ravens still sell well when trained, unusual, or attached to a good omen.
flavorText: “Ravens know too much and charge nothing for the discomfort.”

name: fabled caustic shrike
shortDescription: A fabled caustic shrike claim from a birdlike terror whose bite, spit, or plumage is said to burn more than pride. Poisoners, alchemists, and collectors chase the legend, while cage makers quietly refuse commissions without payment in advance.
flavorText: “A small bird can still ruin a large man's day.”

name: notable cyclops
shortDescription: A notable cyclops token from hill caves, ruined towers, or stories where one eye was enough to spot trouble very early. Stoneworkers, warlords, and monster scholars value the association, especially when the specimen comes with proof larger than rumor.
flavorText: “One eye is plenty when the target is everyone.”

name: market deep mire brute
shortDescription: A market deep mire brute claim, processed into ordinary trade language for an extraordinary marsh threat. Hunters and marsh lords sell such proof to armorers, apothecaries, and nervous border towns that prefer monsters measured before they arrive.
flavorText: “The mire sends brutes; the market sends invoices.”

name: scarce berserker deep mire brute
shortDescription: A scarce berserker deep mire brute token tied to a furious marsh creature known for ruining boats, fences, and confidence. It draws interest from war leaders and monster scholars, though any buyer should inspect the chain marks before believing the story ended neatly.
flavorText: “Some swamp things do not lurk. They charge.”

name: scarce earth mage deep mire brute
shortDescription: A scarce earth mage deep mire brute record, stranger than common brute stock because it suggests mud, stone, and spellcraft working together badly. Mage guilds and frontier wardens both pay attention, since a marsh beast with magic changes every map nearby.
flavorText: “When the mud learns spells, roads become suggestions.”

name: scarce shaman deep mire brute
shortDescription: A scarce shaman deep mire brute token linked to marsh rites, bone charms, and guttural wisdom from places where dry boots are suspicious. Scholars and ward-makers value it, while locals prefer that outsiders stop asking respectful questions at night.
flavorText: “The mire has priests, and they do not bless travelers.”

name: notable double headed giant
shortDescription: A notable double headed giant claim that carries twice the face, twice the argument, and twice the need for verification. Collectors love the spectacle, war colleges love the tactics, and merchants love that one monster can justify two stories.
flavorText: “Two heads make negotiation complicated and shouting unnecessary.”

name: fabled fire giant
shortDescription: A fabled fire giant record from volcanic halls, forge kingdoms, or burned passes where size and heat become politics. Smiths, warlords, and collectors bid for such proof, but every contract includes transport warnings written by people who enjoy living.
flavorText: “A fire giant does not enter a tale quietly; it leaves the margins scorched.”

name: scarce frost giant
shortDescription: A scarce frost giant token tied to high cold territories where footprints fill with snow before fear does. Northern courts and monster scholars prize it, while caravan masters treat the name as a reminder to count blankets before courage.
flavorText: “Frost giants make winter feel like someone owns it.”

name: market hill giant
shortDescription: A market hill giant claim from uplands where boulders, goats, and travelers all learn humility. It is less rare than grander giant stock, but still valuable to towns hiring protection, scholars keeping ledgers, and armorers wanting large material samples.
flavorText: “Hill giants are common only to people not standing nearby.”

name: fabled iron mire brute
shortDescription: A fabled iron mire brute token from marsh legends where mud hardens into something like armor and bad temper. Armorers and wardens both prize the claim, because any creature that survives swamp and metal deserves expensive study.
flavorText: “Iron in the mire rusts. This brute did not.”

name: fabled storm ox colossus
shortDescription: A fabled storm ox colossus record tied to a thunderous horned giant of open plains and black skies. Its legend attracts warlords, priests, and weather mages, all pretending they want knowledge rather than the right to say they touched proof.
flavorText: “The storm grew hooves and learned to lower its head.”

name: scarce minotaur
shortDescription: A scarce minotaur token from maze ruins, old arenas, or border caves where strength and stubbornness share a skull. It is valued by collectors, war schools, and builders of traps who admire how well a corridor can become a weapon.
flavorText: “A minotaur makes architecture feel personally hostile.”

name: market ogre
shortDescription: A market ogre claim, common enough for trade ledgers yet large enough to make handlers careful. Ogres interest labor brokers, monster fighters, and city guards, though most buyers prefer the paperwork to any arrangement involving an actual wagon ride.
flavorText: “An ogre in the market is either a product, a problem, or both.”

name: scarce mage ogre
shortDescription: A scarce mage ogre record, troubling because ogre strength becomes far less predictable when joined with spellcraft. Mage guilds study it, warlords desire it, and practical merchants increase the price for every intact ward found on the crate.
flavorText: “An ogre with magic turns brute force into a theory.”

name: market rock mire brute
shortDescription: A market rock mire brute token from stony marshland where hard ground and sucking mud collaborate badly. It sells to frontier wardens, quarry scouts, and monster scholars as practical warning stock, especially in regions where roads keep disappearing sideways.
flavorText: “Rock, mud, and muscle: three poor ingredients for a peaceful road.”

name: notable ghost mire brute
shortDescription: A notable ghost mire brute record tied to a marsh threat spoken of in whispers, pale sightings, and empty footprints filling with black water. Exorcists and collectors value it highly, while ferrymen simply charge more after hearing the name.
flavorText: “The mire kept the brute and misplaced the body.”

name: scarce stone giant
shortDescription: A scarce stone giant claim from high cliffs and cavern halls where patience is measured in erosion. Builders, war colleges, and collectors seek such proof, though experienced merchants know stone giant goods are heavy even before prestige is added.
flavorText: “A stone giant considers haste a young mineral's problem.”

name: fabled titan
shortDescription: A fabled titan token or encounter claim, so large in reputation that ordinary ownership feels absurd. Rulers, temples, and scholars may still bid for it, because civilization has always paid dearly for proof that something greater once looked down.
flavorText: “A titan is not found. A titan is noticed by history.”

name: market swamp mire brute
shortDescription: A market swamp mire brute claim from wet lowlands where muscle, muck, and bad visibility make honest work expensive. The item is common enough for practical trade, yet dangerous enough that marsh guides smirk whenever inland buyers call it routine.
flavorText: “Swamp brutes are local weather with fists.”

name: notable two headed ogre
shortDescription: A notable two headed ogre record, valuable for spectacle, monster study, and the enduring mystery of whether two bad ideas count as strategy. Collectors pay well, and arena brokers pay better if both heads are known to disagree loudly.
flavorText: “Two heads, one appetite, no improvement in manners.”

name: bogling
shortDescription: A bogling token from marsh villages, reed forts, and damp paths where small folk know the ground better than any invader. It is modest stock, but useful to diplomats, scouts, and anyone trying to trade where maps end in wet guesses.
flavorText: “A bogling is small only to those standing on dry land.”

name: bogling high priest
shortDescription: A bogling high priest record tied to marsh rites, sacred mud, and leadership that makes outsiders uneasy before negotiations begin. Temples and scholars seek it for study, while experienced traders bring respectful gifts and avoid jokes about puddles.
flavorText: “In the bog, holiness leaves footprints that fill with water.”

name: bogling knight
shortDescription: A bogling knight token from reed-armored defenders of wetland paths and hidden settlements. The title sounds small until one sees how badly heavy cavalry performs in mud, which is why border captains value such records more than they admit.
flavorText: “A knight does not need a horse when the ground itself is an ally.”

name: bogling priest
shortDescription: A bogling priest token associated with local rites, water charms, and soft-spoken authority in places where every path must ask the marsh for permission. It is useful to scholars and diplomats, but only respectful merchants keep repeat buyers.
flavorText: “The bog listens better to prayers spoken at knee height.”

name: bogling sorcerer
shortDescription: A bogling sorcerer record linked to reed magic, mist tricks, and spells that make solid ground feel negotiable. Mage guilds want the techniques, scouts want the warnings, and traders want assurance that the crate is not slowly filling with fog.
flavorText: “Bogling sorcery begins where dry logic sinks.”

name: bogling warlord
shortDescription: A bogling warlord token tied to wetland campaigns, reed banners, and small warriors organized into a very large problem. Border lords and military scribes value it, because command in a bog is less about size than knowing where everyone else will sink.
flavorText: “A warlord of the marsh wins by choosing which ground counts as ground.”
